source.title: Hydraulic Shock Safety Bulletin January 2015 - Anhydrous Ammonia Release at Millard Refrigerated Services, Inc.

event.incident_type: Hydraulic shock causing catastrophic piping failure and toxic gas release
actions_taken: Three Millard employees went to the roof to manually close valves to isolate the blast freezer ammonia supply
All other Millard employees evacuated to an upwind location
Operations Manager announced the release on the facility intercom
Plant Manager called 9-1-1 approximately 15 minutes after being notified
Mobile Fire Department responded with hazardous materials technicians
Mobile County Emergency Management Agency advised shelter-in-place for surrounding community
U.S. Coast Guard halted water traffic on the industrial canal
Mobile County Fire and Rescue set up an impromptu triage site approximately 2 miles from the DWH clean-up site
Release secured by 1:20 pm approximately 4 hours after initial release
CDC and ATSDR conducted Assessment of Chemical Exposure survey of affected persons
event.summary: On August 23, 2010, at Millard Refrigerated Services in Theodore, Alabama, a hydraulic shock event caused catastrophic failure of a roof-mounted 12-inch suction pipe and rupture of a blast freezer evaporator coil, releasing approximately 32,100 pounds of anhydrous ammonia. The hydraulic shock was triggered during restart of the ammonia refrigeration system following a 7-hour power outage. A refrigeration system operator manually cleared an alarm, interrupting an in-progress defrost cycle and causing the system to switch directly from defrost to refrigeration mode without bleeding high-pressure hot gas from the evaporator coil. The resulting rapid depressurisation mixed hot gas and cold liquid ammonia, causing condensation-induced shock and vapor-propelled liquid slug events that ruptured the evaporator piping manifold and rooftop suction header. Contributing factors included a software programming error in the control system, multiple evaporator coils grouped to a single set of control valves, insufficient pump-out time, and failure to activate the emergency shutdown switch promptly. The ammonia cloud travelled 0.25 miles across the adjacent canal, exposing over 152 offsite workers and contractors. One Millard employee, 9 ship crew members, and 143 DWH decontamination contractors were exposed; 32 were hospitalised and 4 placed in intensive care.
recommendations: Avoid grouping multiple large-capacity evaporators to a single set of control valves; assign individual valve control groups to each evaporator coil.
Program defrost control systems with interlocks to automatically depressurize or bleed coils upon restart after an outage or interruption, prior to opening the suction stop valve.
Avoid manual interruption of evaporators while in defrost; equip control systems with password-protected controls to restrict manual override to trained and authorized personnel only.
Ensure pump-out times are long enough to remove sufficient residual liquid refrigerant from evaporator coils prior to introducing hot gas, especially after low-load periods or power outages.

In the event of an ammonia release that cannot be promptly isolated, activate the emergency shutdown switch to de-energize pumps, compressors, and valves rather than attempting to isolate leaking equipment while the system is running.

hazards.name: Large inventory of anhydrous ammonia under pressure in refrigeration system
Hydraulic shock potential during hot gas defrost cycle transitions

threats.name: Manual operator interruption of defrost cycle clearing alarm without bleed cycle
Software programming error allowing transition from soft gas directly to refrigeration mode without bleed
Insufficient pump-out time leaving residual liquid ammonia in evaporator coils
Multiple evaporator coils grouped to single control valve set creating excessive hot gas volume
7-hour power outage causing uncontrolled interruption of defrost cycle

consequences.name: Catastrophic failure of roof-mounted 12-inch suction pipe and release of 32,100 lb anhydrous ammonia
Blast freezer evaporator coil rupture and internal ammonia release
Mass casualty ammonia exposure event - 32 hospitalisations, 4 intensive care admissions
Contamination of 8 million pounds of poultry and packaging material
Prolonged ammonia release due to delayed emergency shutdown - release continued approximately 4 hours
Potential for ammonia deflagration if ignition source present

controls.name: Defrost control system bleed/depressurisation sequence before suction stop valve opening
controls.barrier_role: Ensures evaporator coil is depressurised before low-temperature suction valve opens, preventing rapid pressure differential and hydraulic shock
controls.lod_basis: Primary engineered safeguard in the defrost control sequence to prevent hydraulic shock initiation

supporting_text: the operator manually cleared an alarm in the system. This resulted in an interruption of a defrost cycle that was in progress for a blast freezer evaporator. The evaporator switched directly from defrost mode into refrigeration mode without bleeding hot gas from the evaporator coil.

the control system contained a programming error that permitted the system to go from soft gas directly to refrigeration mode without bleeding the high pressure from the coil or preventing the low-temperature suction valve from opening.

Program the defrost control sequence to automatically depressurize or bleed the coil upon restart after an outage or interruption, prior to opening the suction stop valve to set the evaporator into cooling mode.

controls.name: Password-protected control system access restricting manual override to authorised personnel
controls.barrier_role: Prevents unauthorised or untrained personnel from manually interrupting defrost sequences or overriding programmed control logic
controls.lod_basis: Administrative access control as second line of defence against improper manual intervention

supporting_text: This was enabled by a lack of restricted access to control system modifications. Had password-protected controls been in place, they could have been used to restrict access to only authorized personnel trained to modify the refrigeration system sequence and pump-out times.

Avoid the manual interruption of evaporators while in defrost and equip control systems with password protected controls to ensure only trained and authorized personnel have the authority to manually override system processes.

controls.name: Operator training on defrost cycle manual intervention procedures
controls.barrier_role: Ensures operators understand consequences of manually interrupting defrost sequences and are competent to modify control system settings appropriately
controls.lod_basis: Administrative training control to prevent human error during manual system intervention

supporting_text: The probability of improperly sequencing manual defrost increases when workers are not trained on how to appropriately modify the control sequence.
equip control systems with password protected controls to ensure only trained and authorized personnel have the authority to manually override system processes.

controls.name: Adequate pump-out time to clear residual liquid ammonia before hot gas introduction
controls.barrier_role: Removes residual liquid ammonia from evaporator coils prior to hot gas defrost, reducing risk of vapor-propelled liquid slug and hydraulic shock
controls.lod_basis: Operational control as first line of defence against liquid accumulation contributing to hydraulic shock

supporting_text: the blast freezer evaporator defrost timer was originally intended to run the pump-out cycle for 20 minutes; however CSB investigators reviewed control system data for the evaporators and found that it only lasted 15 minutes on the days leading up to the incident.
the power outage, followed by a low-load period in the middle of the night, allowed for the low-temperature liquid ammonia to fill the coils and settle in the bottom evaporator.

For time-initiated hot gas defrost systems, ensure pump-out times are long enough to remove all liquid refrigerant from the evaporator coils prior to introducing hot gas, especially after low-load periods or power outages.

controls.name: Individual control valve groups per evaporator coil (system design)
controls.barrier_role: Limits the volume of high-pressure hot gas that can be introduced to the suction line upon premature valve opening, reducing hydraulic shock severity
controls.lod_basis: Inherently safer design control limiting energy release volume

supporting_text: Millard and the designers of the refrigeration system designed the blast freezer evaporators at the Theodore facility with four evaporator coils grouped to one set of control valves.

Had Millard assigned each evaporator to an individual set of control valves, only 15 cubic feet at 70 psig would have been released to the header when the control system reset signaled the suction stop valve to open. The lower volume of hot gas may have resulted in a minor shock event, such as an audible hammering or shaking, instead of a pipe rupture.
For the design of ammonia refrigeration systems, avoid grouping multiple, large-capacity evaporators to a single set of control valves.

controls.name: Control system software logic validation and testing
controls.barrier_role: Ensures defrost control sequence logic is correct and prevents erroneous transitions between defrost and cooling modes without required bleed steps
controls.lod_basis: Engineering quality assurance control to detect and correct software logic errors before operational deployment

supporting_text: According to Millard documentation, the control system contained a programming error that permitted the system to go from soft gas directly to refrigeration mode without bleeding the high pressure from the coil or preventing the low-temperature suction valve from opening.

The error with the software logic in the control system went undetected because under normal operations, in its programmed sequence, the defrost cycle would not allow the ammonia liquid to enter the evaporator until the coil was properly depressurized via the bleed cycle.

controls.name: Indoor ammonia air-monitoring detection system with alarms
controls.barrier_role: Detects elevated ammonia concentrations inside the facility following a release, triggering alarms to alert personnel and initiate emergency response
controls.lod_basis: First mitigation line of defence - detection and alarm to initiate emergency response after loss of containment

supporting_text: alarms sounded within the plant due to the detection of high concentrations of ammonia indoors by air-monitoring equipment in the freezers.
The ammonia detected within the facility was the result of a second leak that developed because a portion of the system's blast freezer evaporator header ruptured.

controls.name: Emergency shutdown (e-stop) system to de-energize compressors, pumps, and valves
controls.barrier_role: Stops circulation of ammonia through the refrigeration system upon activation, limiting the quantity of ammonia released from failed equipment
controls.lod_basis: Second mitigation line of defence - active engineering safeguard to limit release volume after loss of containment

supporting_text: The use of the emergency stop button (e-stop), located in the Millard control room, would have shut down the compressors and pumps, and de-energized valves. Shutting down these components would have stopped the circulation of ammonia into the other evaporators and decreased the quantity of ammonia that flowed out to the failed suction line located on the roof.

Because the employees attempted to isolate the release to avoid shutting down refrigeration for the entire facility, the release quantity was significantly greater than it would have been had they simply shut the system down.
Millard identified that the e-stop button should have been activated earlier by the Plant Engineer in their internal investigation report of the incident.

In the event of an ammonia release, activate the emergency shut-down switch to de-energize pumps, compressors and valves instead of attempting to isolate leaking equipment while the refrigeration system is running.

controls.name: Emergency response procedure for ammonia release including e-stop activation criteria
controls.barrier_role: Provides structured guidance to personnel on appropriate emergency actions including when to activate emergency shutdown versus attempting manual isolation
controls.lod_basis: Administrative mitigation control guiding emergency response decision-making after loss of containment

supporting_text: The Millard emergency procedure instructed personnel to 'locate the leak' and attempt to isolate the leak. The procedure stated that 'control of an ammonia leak will require that the leak be stopped by valving off a pipe or piece of equipment.'
the plan engineer made the decision to locate and isolate the release instead of shutting down the ammonia system.
The activation of the e-stop would have reduced the volume and the duration of the ammonia release, and lessoned offsite consequences.

controls.name: Facility evacuation and upwind muster procedure
controls.barrier_role: Moves personnel away from ammonia release to safe upwind locations, reducing personnel exposure and injury
controls.lod_basis: Third mitigation line of defence - personnel protection through evacuation after release has occurred

supporting_text: All other Millard employees evacuated the facility to a location upwind from the ammonia release.

After the Operations Manager announced the release on the Millard facility intercom, three Millard employees went onto the roof in an attempt to mitigate the leak by closing manual valves that supplied ammonia to the blast freezer.

controls.name: External emergency response - fire department hazmat and medical triage
controls.barrier_role: Provides hazmat assessment, air monitoring, community shelter-in-place advisory, and medical triage for exposed casualties
controls.lod_basis: Recovery-level mitigation through external emergency services response

supporting_text: the Millard Plant Manager called 9-1-1, triggering a response from the Mobile Fire Department (MFD), which arrived on scene 16 minutes later with hazardous materials technicians to assess and mitigate the release.
The MFD called the Mobile County Emergency Management Agency and advised shelter-in-place for the surrounding community and the U.S. Coast Guard halted water traffic on the industrial canal.
Mobile County Fire and Rescue set up an impromptu triage site at a gas station about 2 miles from the DWH clean-up site where exposed workers gathered upon evacuation.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
notes.analyst_notes: PIF references in controls use informal IDs (PIF-001 through PIF-005) as placeholders: PIF-001=operator competence/training on defrost manual intervention; PIF-002=situational awareness of system state after power outage; PIF-003=organisational training programme adequacy; PIF-004=plant engineer situational awareness during emergency; PIF-005=emergency procedure adequacy and decision-making under pressure. The incident involved both prevention barrier failures (bleed cycle, software logic, pump-out time, system design) and a mitigation barrier failure (delayed e-stop activation). The software programming error (C-006) represents a latent organisational failure that was only exposed under the abnormal post-outage restart condition.
